text: The surgeon locates the most mobile loop of ileum that can reach the urethra without tension, a crucial modification in the technique for ileocolic resection.
The surgeon identifies the specific point on the ileum that will be used for the urethroileal anastomosis, marking the location for future connection to the urethra.
The surgeon marks all portions of the neobladder with 3-0 undyed Vicryl sutures, indicating the areas for subsequent surgical manipulation.
The surgeon isolates an 11-centimeter segment of bowel distal to the ileocecal valve, marking it as the zero-centimeter point for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon uses an eleven-centimeter segment of bowel to measure the remaining proximal portions of the small bowel for constructing the neobladder.
The surgeon marks bowel portions not used for the diversion with 3-0 dyed vicryl sutures, distinguishing them from the segments allocated for the neobladder construction.
At the thirty-three centimeter point, the surgeon identifies additional bowel segments located below and behind the eleven centimeter point, marked for the procedure.
The surgeon reaches the forty-four centimeter point on the bowel, marking the progression of the surgical measurement and isolation process.
The surgeon marks the end of the bowel segment required for forming the pouch, indicating the completion of the necessary bowel isolation for the procedure.
The surgeon measures the discard segment of bowel and divides it using a 60mm laparoscopic stapler, then removes the segment from the surgical field.
The surgeon locates the portions of the ileum that will be anastomosed, marked by previously placed dyed two-zero Vicryl sutures.
The surgeon highlights two dyed blue stitches on the bowel, indicating the points for anastomosis.
The surgeon aligns the intestinal segments for an intracorporeal side-to-side anastomosis, using a 60mm laparoscopic stapler to join the bowel sections.
The fourth robotic arm retracts the apex of the posterior plate toward the pelvis as a 24 French chest tube is inserted into the isolated intestine. The bowel is then detubularized.
The surgeon makes a bias incision toward the medial mesenteric edge of the intestine using electrocautery, creating a precise cut along the mesenteric border.
The surgeon aligns the medial edges of the bivalve intestine and places interrupted sutures to maintain symmetry of the posterior plate, using stay sutures for handling.

The assistant and the fourth robotic arm hold the barbed sutures under tension, aligning the edges of the intestinal tissue to ensure a watertight seal with each stitch. The sutures are visibly six inches long to aid in intracorporeal handling.

The surgeon approximates the edges of the bowel using running sutures in a distal-to-proximal direction, starting from the apex of the posterior plate towards the chimney, employing a 2-0 V-lock stitch on a CT-1 needle.
The surgeon rotates the posterior plate of the neobladder ninety degrees counterclockwise, aligning the urethro-ileal anastomosis point with the urethra.

The surgeon sutures the previously placed barbed stitch at the urethral ileal anastomosis point to the distal Denonvilliers fascia, adjacent to the recto urethralis muscle, to reduce tension on the anastomosis.
The surgeon begins the urethral ileal anastomosis by suturing the edge of the ileum to the posterior six o'clock position of the urethral stump, ensuring precise alignment of the tissues.
The surgeon continues the urethral ileal anastomosis anteriorly in a running fashion, suturing over a 22 French Cavalier urethral catheter, progressing towards the twelve o'clock position.
The surgeon places interrupted sutures to maintain symmetry in the surgical field, using stay sutures for handling. Each suture is visibly six inches long, facilitating intracorporeal manipulation.

The surgeon folds the lateral edges of the posterior plate anteriorly and closes them with a running suture using a 2-0 V-Loc stitch on a CT-1 needle, progressing towards the chimney. The assistant holds the sutures to align the edges and ensure a watertight seal, contributing to the spherical configuration of the pouch.
The surgeon brings the left ureter to the right side, positioning it under the sigmoid colon. The distal ends of the ureters, previously clipped, are now resected.
The surgeon tailors the lengths of the ureters to match the anastomosis location at the chimney, and the ureteral ends are spatulated to prepare for connection.
The surgeon creates a small enterotomy in the afferent limb and performs a brick-like anastomosis, aligning the bowel segments for connection.

The surgeon performs the anastomosis using a 4-0 Vicryl suture with an RB-1 needle, applying running sutures over a 7 French double J stent. The stent is inserted through a 2mm mini port temporarily placed in the right inferior quadrant to aid in stent placement.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 Vicryl running suture to oversew the proximal end of the ureteral chimney, ensuring the staple line is excluded from contact with urine and mucus to prevent stone formation.
The surgeon fills the neobladder pouch with 180 milliliters of saline, visually confirming a watertight closure with no leakage.
The surgeon considers using indocyanine green (ICG) to assess the vascularity of the neobladder, which would involve visualizing blood flow to ensure adequate perfusion.
